1: Job’s opening challenge — “Why are not times kept by the Almighty, and why do those who know him never see his days?” — poses a precise theological problem. The “times” (ittim) refers to the divinely appointed moments for justice to be enacted; the “days” of the Almighty are his days of judgment (cf. “the day of YHWH” in the prophets). Job observes that God does not keep his own appointed schedule for justice: social wickedness continues unchecked, and God’s intervention does not come when expected. The Psalter echoes this complaint: Psalms 10:1 (“why do you stand far off, O LORD? Why do you hide yourself in times of trouble?”); Psalms 74:9–11 (“how long, O God?”).
2: The moving of boundary stones (gebulot) was the most serious form of agrarian fraud in the ancient Near East. Boundary stones (kudurru in Akkadian) were stone monuments planted to record land ownership; in Mesopotamia they were inscribed with elaborate divine curse formulae against anyone who moved, defaced, or destroyed them, and were placed under divine protection (typically the sun-god Shamash, patron of justice). Deuteronomy 27:17: “Cursed be anyone who moves his neighbor’s landmark.” Proverbs 22:28: “Do not move an ancient landmark that your fathers have set.” In a world without written deeds or land registries, the physical boundary stone was the only record of land ownership for agricultural families.
3: The theft of orphans’ donkeys and widows’ oxen targets the two classes of people in the ancient world who had no male advocate in legal proceedings. The paterfamilias represented the household in all legal and commercial matters; a widow or orphan without a male kinsman had no effective recourse against theft. The prophetic indictments of Israel repeatedly identified exploitation of the widow, orphan, and poor as the paradigmatic social sin (Isaiah 1:17,23; Job 10:2; Amos 2:6–7; Micah 2:1–2; Jeremiah 7:6; Ezekiel 22:7). The ANE legal codes similarly recognized special protections for these vulnerable groups: the preamble to Hammurabi’s Code identifies protecting the widow and orphan as a primary royal obligation.
4: The poor being pushed off their road (yattu ’aniyyim miderech) reflects the ancient right-of-way conventions for the marginalized. In ancient Near Eastern cities, public paths, water sources, and market access were essential for the survival of the poor; exclusion from these constituted economic death. The phrase “the poor of the earth hide themselves together” pictures an underclass forced into collective concealment from their oppressors — a social reality documented in ancient administrative texts where the landless poor gathered in marginal areas outside settled zones.
5: The comparison of the dispossessed poor to pere’ (wild donkeys) “in the desert” (ba’aravah) echoes the birth oracle of Ishmael in Genesis 16:12: “he will be a wild donkey of a man; his hand against everyone and everyone’s hand against him, and he will live to the east of all his kinsmen.” Both texts use the wild donkey as the paradigm of marginal existence outside the social structures of settled life. In the ancient world, the wild donkey (onager) ranged the desert steppe beyond the zone of agricultural settlement; to be compared to one was to be identified with those who existed outside the social order that guaranteed identity, security, and rights.
6: The poor gathering fodder (vequtsr) in a field that belongs to another is a picture of the ancient practice of gleaning — collecting the leftover grain after the formal harvest. Mosaic Law mandated that landowners leave the edges of fields and fallen grain for the poor and sojourner (Leviticus 19:9–10; Job 23:22; Deuteronomy 24:19–21). The book of Ruth portrays this gleaning practice as a survival mechanism for widows. Job’s description shows the poor reduced to gleaning in the vineyard of the wicked — subsisting on the margins of the economy of those who exploit them.
7: The naked poor lying without clothing through the night is one of the OT’s most direct descriptions of destitution. In the Mediterranean climate, nights in the hill country could be cold; the outer garment taken as a pledge (cf. Exodus 22:26–27; Deuteronomy 24:12–13) was the poor person’s only protection against the cold. The legislation requiring the return of a pledge before nightfall was precisely because the garment served as a blanket. Job’s description of people who have no covering in the cold and no clothing to cover themselves depicts the lived consequence of the pledge-taking abuse Eliphaz falsely accused Job of committing (Job 22:6).
8: Being “wet with mountain rains and cling to the rock for want of shelter” is a vivid description of exposure to the Palestinian climate. The annual rain season (October–April) included downpours; without shelter, exposure to rain in the hill country produced hypothermia. The rock as last resort shelter echoes the wilderness imagery throughout the Psalter (Psalms 18:2: YHWH as rock, fortress, refuge). The irony in Job’s description is that the things YHWH provides (rock as metaphorical shelter) are the literal last resort of those whom society has failed to shelter.
9: The snatching of orphans from the breast reflects the practice of child-bondage for debt. When a debtor defaulted, creditors could seize children as debt-slaves in the ancient world. 2 Kings 4:1–7 provides a direct OT example: a widow whose husband had died in debt was about to have her two sons taken by the creditor. In Mesopotamian law, debt-slavery was regulated (Hammurabi Code, Laws 117–119): a debtor who sold himself or family members into slavery was limited to a three-year term. The OT regulated but did not eliminate the practice (Exodus 21:7–11; Deuteronomy 15:12–17). Job describes the unregulated, extortionate version of this practice.
10: The poor “go about naked, without clothing; hungry, they carry the sheaves” — the phrase captures the ultimate irony of agrarian exploitation: the people who perform the harvest labor that produces food are themselves the most food-insecure. In ancient Egypt, the peasant farmers who produced the grain stored in pharaoh’s granaries could face starvation when those granaries were controlled by the state. The Biblical Joseph story (Genesis 47:13–26) describes how this system worked: all Egyptian grain eventually belonged to pharaoh. Job’s description shows the same dynamic in a private-exploitation context.
11: The laborers who produce olive oil between the millstones but go thirsty, who tread the winepress but suffer thirst, depicts the grotesque inversion at the heart of agricultural exploitation. In the ancient Near East, olive oil and wine were the premium agricultural products and the primary sources of surplus wealth; they were also the staples of diet (with bread). The workers who produce these products while denied their consumption suffered the deepest form of material injustice: proximity to abundance with enforced deprivation. The Mosaic Law’s prohibition on muzzling an ox while it treads grain (Deuteronomy 25:4) encoded a principle of minimal worker access to the produce of their labor.
12: The urban context — “from out of the city the dying groan, and the soul of the wounded cries for help” — shifts from agricultural to urban suffering. The “cry” (tisha’vena) of the wounded is the tzeaqah (cry of distress/injustice) — the category of cry that activates divine response in the OT: Genesis 4:10 (Abel’s blood cries from the ground); Exodus 3:7 (God hears the Israelites’ tzeaqah in Egypt); Exodus 22:23 (widows and orphans cry and God will hear). The piercing theological irony of v12 is the final phrase: “yet God charges no one with wrongdoing (vel’o–yasim ’elohim tifla`h)” — God remains silent despite hearing the cry that should activate his judgment.
13: The section on “those who rebel against the light” (vv13–17) introduces the ancient moral coding of darkness and light. Throughout the ancient world, darkness was the time of social dissolution, moral transgression, and the activity of forces hostile to civilization. Mesopotamian texts associate night with the activities of demons and the underworld. In the OT, nighttime crimes are morally coded as especially sinister because they exploit the darkness to avoid divine observation (cf. Job 22:13–14 where Eliphaz attributed to Job the idea that God cannot see). The three nighttime criminals of vv14–17 — the murderer, adulterer, thief — all share the characteristic of exploiting darkness.
14: The murderer rising at dawn to kill the poor and needy (“with the light the murderer rises; he kills the poor and needy, and in the night he is like a thief”) inverts the associations of morning and safety. Dawn (shahar) throughout the Psalter represents divine deliverance (Psalms 46:5: “God will help her when morning dawns”; Psalms 30:5: “joy comes with the morning”). For the poor, dawn is instead the signal for the murderer who kills them in the transitional moment when the night-thief becomes the day-murderer. The category of “poor and needy” (’ani ve’evyon) as murder victims reflects the ancient reality that the poor had no armed protection.
15: The adulterer who “watches for the twilight, saying, No eye will see me, and he veils his face” reflects the ancient understanding of adultery as a crime of concealment and darkness. In the ANE legal codes, adultery was severely punished (death in Hammurabi Code, Laws 129–132; Leviticus 20:10; Deuteronomy 22:22). The crime required secrecy precisely because of the severe penalties; the veil that hides the adulterer’s face is the instrument of the denial that all adultery depends upon. The phrase “no eye will see me” echoes Eliphaz’s accusation that Job thought the divine eye could not see through the clouds (Job 22:13–14), completing the ironic inversion: the wicked ACTUALLY hold this view, while Job does not.
16: Thieves who “break into houses in the dark; in the daytime they seal themselves up” reflects the ancient categorization of burglary as a nighttime crime. In Hammurabi’s Code (Law 21), breaking into a house was punishable by death, with execution at the site of entry. The house-breaking (hoter bayit) image appears in Exodus 22:2 in the distinction between nighttime and daytime burglary (the homeowner may kill a nighttime burglar without guilt; not so in daylight). The criminals of Job 24:14–16 illustrate precisely the class of people who exploit darkness for their crimes — the same light-haters of v13.
17: The light-haters’ fellowship with the terrors of thick darkness (ki yaqd hoshekh lam yoter kitsalmavet, deep darkness is morning to them) inverts the normal diurnal orientation. In ANE cosmology, the transition between night and day was a moment of divine ordering (the sun-god Shamash’s daily journey was a renewal of cosmic order); the morning was the time of divine triumph over the forces of night/chaos. For the criminals of vv13–17, this diurnal order is exactly reversed: they fear the light and “know” darkness as their natural element. This reversal of the light/dark moral coding is one of the ways the ancient world identified the wicked as fundamentally disordered.
18: The textual and interpretive difficulties of vv18–25 have generated extensive scholarly discussion. Many commentators consider these verses as voiced by one of the friends (perhaps quoting standard wisdom doctrine) or as a misplaced text. In the flow of Job’s speech, the description of the swift destruction of the wicked (v18: “swift is he on the face of the waters”) stands in tension with the preceding description of the wicked’s apparent impunity (vv1–17). The verse may represent Job quoting or partially ironically adopting the friends’ position before refuting it, a literary device attested in ancient wisdom dialogue.
19: The agricultural metaphor — drought and heat consume the snow waters, so Sheol consumes those who have sinned — uses the natural process of seasonal water-loss as an analogy for the irreversible movement toward death. In the ancient world, the evaporation and absorption of water (whether by sun, heat, or dry ground) was understood as total loss — water could not be recovered once evaporated. The comparison of Sheol’s consumption of sinners to this irreversible natural process implies the finality and completeness of divine judgment: once consumed, nothing remains.
20: The picture of the wicked forgotten by their mother’s womb, consumed by worms, no longer remembered, broken like a tree, reflects the ancient understanding of social memory as a form of ongoing existence. In Mesopotamian theology, the dead were sustained by the libations and food-offerings of descendants; to die without descendants, or to be forgotten, was a second death beyond physical death. The inscription of names in royal records and temple archives was an attempt to defeat this second death. Job’s description of the wicked being utterly forgotten — by family, by community, by earth itself — is the ultimate negation of legacy.
21: The identification of the wicked as those who exploit the barren woman (who cannot produce heirs) and do no good for the widow connects the fate of the wicked back to the social crimes of vv2–9. The “barren woman” (’aqarah) in the ancient world had no social standing through children; the widow without sons had no legal advocate. Both were paradigmatic victims of exploitation in the ancient Near Eastern legal and social world. The wicked of Job 24 are those who target precisely these structurally vulnerable members of society, and their own social extinction — being forgotten, without descendants — is presented as a fitting mirror-judgment.
22: The mighty who are dragged away (“God drags away the mighty by his power”) despite their accumulated strength (abilim) uses the vocabulary of the divine warrior who overcomes human power. In ANE royal inscriptions, the great kings boasted of their invincible strength; the OT consistently argues that divine power overturns the mightiest human powers (cf. 1 Samuel 2:4: “the bows of the mighty are broken, but the feeble bind on strength”; Psalms 46:9: “he breaks the bow and shatters the spear”). Job appears to describe the eventual divine overthrow of the powerful wicked, but in a way that seems to contradict the sustained divine inaction of vv1–17.
23: The paradox of vv23–24 is striking: God gives the wicked security and rest (betah), his eyes are on their ways, they are exalted for a little while, then destroyed. This precisely encapsulates the problem of the prosperity of the wicked that the Psalter wrestles with (Psalms 73:3–12: Asaph nearly loses his faith seeing the ease of the wicked; their feet stand on slippery places and they are destroyed in a moment, v18–19). The “little while” (me’at) of the wicked’s exaltation followed by their humiliation is the temporal solution to the retributive-justice problem: justice is delayed but certain.
24: The wicked being “brought low and gathered like all others; cut off like the heads of grain” uses the harvest-grain metaphor for death that appears throughout biblical and ANE literature. The Mesopotamian image of the god cutting down humans like grain (the Descent of Ishtar: those who die descend to the underworld where the grain is their food) and the OT image of death as reaping (Isaiah 17:5; Jeremiah 9:22; Amos 7:17) both treat the agricultural metaphor of cutting as the universal fate of humanity. The wicked’s fate in v24 — cut down like grain — is distinguished only by timing, not ultimate destiny.
25: Job’s concluding challenge — “If it is not so, who will prove me a liar and make my speech amount to nothing?” — is a formal legal challenge to the friends’ refutation. The phrase ’im-lo mi yazimeni veyasem le’efes millatiy is a juridical formula calling for a counter-argument. In ancient Hebrew legal discourse, a statement followed by “who can prove me wrong?” was a claim to have stated a demonstrably verifiable position. Job is not expressing private theological opinion but stating what he presents as observable social fact: the wicked prosper, the innocent suffer, and God does not intervene on schedule. This is the empirical challenge that demands a response.
